question: Show that the set S defined by ∈ S and s + t ∈ S whenever s ∈ S and t ∈ S is the set of positive integers.
response: "It looks like the definition of the set ( S ) is missing a key part (what element is initially in ( S )). Typically, this kind of problem is stated as:> Let ( S \subseteq \mathbb{Z}^+ ) be a set such that:>> 1. ( 1 \in S ), and> 2. If ( s \in S ) and ( t \in S ), then ( s + t \in S ).>    Show that ( S ) is the set of all positive integers.### ProofWe want to show that ( S = \mathbb{Z}^+ ), i.e., ( S = {1, 2, 3, \dots} ).---### Step 1: Show all positive integers are in ( S )We proceed by induction.**Base case:**We are given that ( 1 \in S ).**Inductive step:**Assume ( k \in S ) for some positive integer ( k ).Since ( 1 \in S ) and ( k \in S ), the closure property gives:[k + 1 \in S.]Thus, if ( k \in S ), then ( k+1 \in S ).By induction, every positive integer ( n \in S ).---### Step 2: Conclude equalityWe have shown:* ( S \subseteq \mathbb{Z}^+ ) (implicitly, since we are building from 1 using addition),* ( \mathbb{Z}^+ \subseteq S ) (from the induction argument).Therefore,[S = \mathbb{Z}^+.]---### ConclusionThe only set containing 1 and closed under addition is the set of all positive integers."
